name: Newcastle
short_description: BLM public rockhounding site near Newcastle, Utah offering agate, jasper, and chalcedony collecting in Iron County high desert terrain. Free access, no permits required.
description:
Newcastle sits in southwestern Utah's high desert landscape, where the Iron County BLM manages accessible public land ideal for recreational rockhounding. This area benefits from the region's rich geological history of silica-rich mineral deposition, resulting in excellent agate and jasper specimens found throughout washes and hillsides. The terrain is typical of the Great Basin transition zone - open, sagebrush-dotted hillsides with scattered volcanic and sedimentary outcrops.

The site is particularly known for finding colorful agate nodules and banded chalcedony pieces in the shallow washes and around volcanic tuff exposures. Winter and spring rains expose new material, and the relatively low elevation keeps the area accessible most of the year. Rockhounders appreciate Newcastle's proximity to other Iron County collecting sites and the straightforward BLM access rules.

Expect moderate walking across uneven terrain with scattered rocks and occasional loose footing. The landscape is open with minimal shade, so sun exposure and heat are factors during warmer months. This location suits intermediate rockhounders comfortable with hillside walking and small-scale surface collecting.
history: Newcastle lies within the Basin and Range province where Miocene-age volcanic activity and subsequent weathering of tuff deposits created silica-rich environments favorable for agate and chalcedony formation. The region's geothermal activity millions of years ago deposited microcrystalline quartz minerals that solidified into the nodules and seams found throughout the area today.
tips: Search the shallow washes and eroded gullies where water has naturally concentrated heavier specimens - look for banded or reddish agate nodules and translucent chalcedony pieces. Use a small pry bar to carefully extract specimens from surrounding matrix, but focus on surface and loose material first. The best color saturation typically appears on freshly exposed specimens in wash bottoms after seasonal runoff, so time your visit for late winter or early spring.
terrain_notes: Elevation approximately 5,800 to 6,200 feet. Open sagebrush-covered hillsides with scattered volcanic and sedimentary outcrops. Uneven, rocky terrain with loose volcanic rock underfoot. Washes and small canyons provide the most productive searching areas. Typical walking distance from parking is 0.5 to 2 miles, depending on target area. Footing requires sturdy boots due to loose rock and occasional sharp volcanic material.
best_season: October - April. The high desert location experiences extreme heat (often exceeding 85 degrees F) from May through September, and afternoon thunderstorms in summer can cause flash flooding in washes. Winter temperatures are mild at this elevation, typically 40-55 degrees F, making collecting comfortable. Spring (March-April) offers good conditions and exposed material from winter rains.
written_directions: From Cedar City, take US 56 east toward Newcastle for approximately 20 miles. Look for BLM land access points along or near Newcastle Road and local forest service roads. The Newcastle townsite area and surrounding hills offer multiple informal parking areas. From Newcastle town, explore the hillsides immediately north and east of the community on accessible BLM land. A high-clearance vehicle is helpful for rough access roads, though passenger vehicles can reach main collecting areas via maintained approaches.
road_conditions: Mix of paved highway and graded dirt/gravel BLM access roads. Some rougher two-track roads may require high-clearance vehicles. Main approaches are passable by standard passenger vehicles.
parking_notes: Informal parking at pullouts along Newcastle Road and near the townsite. No developed parking facilities. Multiple access points allow flexible positioning depending on target collecting areas.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or power tools. Surface collecting and hand tools only. Dig only small test holes and fill them; do not leave large excavations. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or power tools
Hand tools only
Fill all test holes
Pack out all trash
Stay on public BLM land
nearest_services: Cedar City, approximately 25 miles west. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and supplies. Newcastle has minimal services.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with loose volcanic material requires sturdy footwear and reasonable physical fitness. No wheelchair access due to rough, sloping terrain. Moderate walking distance (0.5-2 miles) from parking to productive areas. Open terrain offers sun exposure; limited shade available. Not suitable for visitors with mobility limitations.

faq.question: What types of agate and jasper can I find rockhounding at Newcastle, Utah?
faq.answer: Newcastle offers colorful banded agate, translucent to opaque chalcedony, and various jasper specimens. The agate nodules range from reddish and brown tones to lighter, banded patterns. Chalcedony pieces often show subtle color variation and good polish potential. Specimens typically range from marble-sized to fist-sized, with occasional larger nodules found in productive washes.

faq.question: Is Newcastle, Utah free rockhounding or do I need to pay fees?
faq.answer: Newcastle is completely free rockhounding on BLM public land. No fees, permits, or reservations are required. You can collect up to 25 pounds per day for personal use under standard BLM regulations. This makes it an excellent budget-friendly option for Utah rockhounders.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Newcastle, Utah?
faq.answer: October through April is optimal. Winter and early spring temperatures (40-55 degrees F) are comfortable for hiking and collecting, and winter rains expose fresh material in washes. Avoid May through September when temperatures exceed 85 degrees and afternoon thunderstorms can cause dangerous flash flooding in washes.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring for Newcastle rockhounding?
faq.answer: Pack a small pry bar or rock hammer for careful extraction, a collecting bag or bucket for specimens, sturdy hiking boots for rocky terrain, plenty of water (high desert sun), sunscreen, and a hat. Bring a hand lens to inspect material before collecting. Leave power tools and motorized equipment at home - hand tools only on BLM land.

faq.question: Is Newcastle, Utah good for beginner rockhounders?
faq.answer: Yes, Newcastle is beginner-friendly. Surface specimens are abundant and easily spotted, requiring no special knowledge to locate decent agates and chalcedony. The terrain is moderately challenging but navigable for anyone comfortable hiking on uneven ground. BLM public access eliminates permit complexity, making it an ideal first rockhounding destination.

faq.question: How far is Newcastle from Cedar City and what should I expect there?
faq.answer: Newcastle is approximately 25 miles east of Cedar City via US 56. Cedar City offers complete services including gas, food, lodging, restaurants, and supply shops. Plan to visit Cedar City for supplies before heading to Newcastle. Newcastle town itself has minimal amenities, so arrive prepared with water and snacks.

meta_title: Newcastle Rockhounding - Utah BLM Agate & Jasper
meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding at Newcastle, Utah. Collect agate, jasper, and chalcedony. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit. October-April best season.
